displayName: matter.js 2D Physics Game and Toy Guidelines
description: Quality and correctness rules for offline single-file matter.js 2D rigid-body physics mini-apps (plinko, toppling towers, marble runs, dominoes, ragdolls, catapults, bouncy-ball pits, gravity sandboxes). Covers the classic UMD script-tag load contract (no ESM under file://), building bodies with the Composite/Bodies/Constraint helpers, the fixed-timestep Runner, rendering to a viewport-sized canvas, capping the body count, mouse and touch dragging via MouseConstraint, and shipping a complete interactive experience. For matter.js physics mini-apps only; do not leak into three.js 3D games or productivity apps.

guidelines.rule: Load matter.js with a classic UMD <script src> tag that exposes the global Matter. NEVER use an ESM 'import Matter from "matter-js"' / <script type=module>. ESM modules silently fail to load under file:// in the WKWebView preview, leaving a blank, dead canvas.
guidelines.why: These mini-apps ship as static single-file bundles and run under file:// inside WKWebView, which is not a secure context. A bare module import or an importmap referencing matter-js does not resolve there and Matter is never defined. The UMD build attaches Matter to window, which works offline under file://.
examples: GOOD: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/matter-js@0.19.0/build/matter.min.js"></script> then const { Engine, Render, ... } = Matter;
WRONG: import Matter from 'matter-js'; - ESM does not resolve under file:// in the preview
WRONG: <script type="module"> import { Engine } from 'matter-js'; </script> - blank canvas

guidelines.rule: Pin a specific matter.js version in the CDN URL (for example matter-js@0.19.0). The platform vendors the referenced version offline; an unversioned URL still works but a pinned one is reproducible and guarantees the vendored copy matches the API you coded against.
guidelines.why: The offline vendoring step fetches the exact version named in the script src. Pinning keeps the shipped bundle deterministic and avoids a 'latest' URL drifting against the module aliases (Engine, Render, Runner, Bodies, Composite) you destructure.
examples: GOOD: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/matter-js@0.19.0/build/matter.min.js"></script>
AVOID: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/matter-js/build/matter.min.js"></script> - unversioned

guidelines.rule: Build bodies and the world with the official module helpers, not by hand: Matter.Bodies (rectangle, circle, polygon, trapezoid), Matter.Composite (add/remove, allBodies), Matter.Composites (stack, pyramid, newtonsCradle), Matter.Constraint / Matter.Composites.chain for joints, and Matter.Body for impulses. Destructure them once from Matter at the top.
guidelines.why: The helpers produce correctly-formed bodies with proper vertices, inertia, and collision data; hand-built body objects break the solver. Matter.Composites.stack/pyramid build towers and brick walls in one call, and Constraint/chain build slingshots, marble-run linkages, and ragdoll joints correctly.
examples: const { Engine, Render, Runner, Bodies, Composite, Constraint, Mouse, MouseConstraint } = Matter;
tower: const stack = Composites.stack(x, y, cols, rows, 0, 0, (px,py) => Bodies.rectangle(px,py,40,40));
ragdoll: link parts with Constraint between body anchor points

guidelines.rule: Step the engine with the official Matter.Runner (fixed timestep), do NOT call Engine.update on a raw requestAnimationFrame with a variable delta. Create a Runner with Runner.create() and Runner.run(runner, engine); it advances the engine at a stable fixed step regardless of frame rate.
guidelines.why: matter's solver is tuned for a roughly fixed 16.6ms step; feeding it a wildly variable rAF delta makes bodies tunnel through walls, jitter, or explode. The Runner provides the stable timestep the solver expects, giving identical, deterministic physics across 30/60/120fps devices and after tab stalls.
examples: const runner = Runner.create(); Runner.run(runner, engine);
WRONG: function loop(){ Engine.update(engine, performance.now()-last); requestAnimationFrame(loop); } with raw deltas
stop with Runner.stop(runner) on teardown

guidelines.rule: Render to a canvas sized to the viewport and keep it responsive. If using Matter.Render, set render.options.width/height to the window size and pixelRatio to Math.min(devicePixelRatio,2); on resize update the canvas size, render bounds, and the boundary walls. Many polished toys instead use Matter as the physics engine only and draw the bodies on their own canvas/Pixi/p5 layer.
guidelines.why: A fixed-size physics canvas leaves dead margins and the boundary walls end up off-screen after a resize, so bodies fall out of view. Sizing the renderer and walls to the viewport (and clamping pixelRatio to 2) keeps the scene full-bleed and performant. Using Matter for physics + a custom draw layer gives full control over the look.
examples: Render.create({ element, engine, options:{ width:innerWidth, height:innerHeight, pixelRatio:Math.min(devicePixelRatio,2), wireframes:false, background:'transparent' } });
on resize: set render.canvas width/height + render.options + reposition the floor/wall bodies
CSS: html,body{margin:0;overflow:hidden} canvas{display:block}

guidelines.rule: Cap the total body count and remove bodies that fall out of bounds. Keep the live body count bounded (a few hundred is plenty for most toys), and when a body falls below the floor or off-screen, Composite.remove it (or recycle it) instead of letting dead bodies accumulate. Lower the cap on mobile.
guidelines.why: The constraint solver cost grows with body count and contacts; thousands of bodies drop even desktops below 30fps and crush mobile. Bouncy-pit, plinko, and domino toys leak bodies that pile up off-screen unless culled. A bounded, culled population keeps the physics smooth.
examples: const MAX_BODIES = (innerWidth < 700) ? 120 : 300;
Events.on(engine, 'afterUpdate', () => Composite.allBodies(engine.world).forEach(b => { if (b.position.y > cullY) Composite.remove(engine.world, b); }));
WRONG: spawning balls forever with no cap and no cull

guidelines.rule: Enclose the play area with static boundary walls so nothing escapes: add static (isStatic:true) floor, ceiling, and side-wall rectangles sized to the viewport, and reposition/resize them on window resize. Use a thick floor so fast bodies do not tunnel through it.
guidelines.why: Without boundary walls every ball, block, and marble falls forever out of view and the toy looks empty within seconds. Static walls sized to the viewport keep the action on screen. A thin floor lets a fast body tunnel through in one step, so make the floor thick (or set a higher solver constraintIterations).
examples: const floor = Bodies.rectangle(w/2, h-20, w, 80, { isStatic:true });
left/right/ceiling walls as static rectangles; reposition all four on resize
WRONG: only a floor, so bodies roll off the sides forever

guidelines.rule: Wire dragging with Matter.MouseConstraint so users can grab and fling bodies on BOTH mouse and touch. Create Mouse.create(render.canvas) and a MouseConstraint, add it to the world, and set mouse.pixelRatio to match the renderer. Apply the recommended touch fix so touch-drag works and does not scroll the page.
guidelines.why: MouseConstraint gives natural grab-and-throw interaction (the core of ragdoll, catapult, marble-run, and sandbox toys) and it already supports touch. Without the standard touch-event handling, touch-drag either does nothing or scrolls the page instead of grabbing a body. The pixelRatio must match the renderer or the grab point is offset.
examples: const mouse = Mouse.create(render.canvas); const mc = MouseConstraint.create(engine, { mouse, constraint:{ stiffness:0.2 } }); Composite.add(world, mc);
mouse.pixelRatio = render.options.pixelRatio; remove the default mousewheel listeners if they hijack scroll
CSS: canvas{touch-action:none} so touch-drag grabs a body instead of scrolling

guidelines.rule: Tune materials for satisfying physics: set restitution (bounciness), friction, and density on bodies so the toy feels right - bouncy balls want high restitution (~0.8), dominoes/towers want low restitution and enough friction to stack, marbles want moderate friction. Adjust engine.gravity if a slower/floatier feel is wanted.
guidelines.why: Default material values feel dead (no bounce) or chaotic. A bouncy-pit with restitution 0 is just a sad pile; dominoes with high restitution never stay stacked. Tuning restitution/friction/density per toy is what makes the physics feel intentional and fun, which is the whole point of a physics toy.
examples: bouncy ball: Bodies.circle(x,y,r,{ restitution:0.85, friction:0.02 });
domino: Bodies.rectangle(x,y,12,60,{ restitution:0.05, friction:0.6 });
floaty toy: engine.gravity.y = 0.5;

guidelines.rule: Use collision events for scoring and feedback. Listen with Matter.Events.on(engine, 'collisionStart', ...) to detect a ball landing in a plinko slot, a block being knocked off, a domino tipping, etc., and update score/UI and trigger effects there. Identify bodies via a label or custom property you set at creation.
guidelines.why: Plinko slots, catapult demolition scoring, and domino counters all need to know when specific bodies touch. collisionStart with labelled bodies is the correct, reliable hook; polling positions every frame is fragile. This is what turns a physics sandbox into a scored, completable game.
examples: Events.on(engine, 'collisionStart', ev => ev.pairs.forEach(p => { if (isSlot(p.bodyA)||isSlot(p.bodyB)) addScore(); }));
tag bodies: Bodies.circle(x,y,r,{ label:'puck' });
use the score to drive a win state and a Reset/Drop button

guidelines.rule: Clean up on teardown: Runner.stop(runner), Render.stop(render) (if used), Engine.clear(engine), World/Composite.clear, remove the canvas and the resize/pointer listeners. Do this when the mini-app restarts or the view is torn down.
guidelines.why: Mini-apps are torn down and relaunched repeatedly in the host. A leftover running Runner, leftover event listeners, or an uncleared engine leaks CPU and bodies across restarts and can run two simulations at once. Explicit cleanup keeps restarts clean.
examples: function teardown(){ Runner.stop(runner); Render.stop(render); Engine.clear(engine); window.removeEventListener('resize', onResize); }
a Reset button should clear and rebuild the world rather than stacking a second one

guidelines.rule: Ship a complete, interactive experience on the first load - the scene is already populated (pegs, tower, dominoes, balls) and the core action works immediately (a Drop/Go/Launch control plus tap-to-act). For GAME goals (plinko, catapult, tower-topple), wire a visible score and a reachable win/lose or clear state with a working Reset, not just an idle pile of bodies.
guidelines.why: The render gate and taste rubric can pass a loaded-but-inert canvas. The product is a working physics toy or game. A plinko board with no way to drop, or a catapult with no targets/score, reads as broken even though it 'loaded'. The satisfying action plus a score and reset is what makes it feel finished.
examples: plinko: peg field + slots present; a Drop button and tap-to-drop-here; score climbs on landing
catapult: slingshot loaded, castle and targets standing; drag-aim-release; score + retry
WRONG: a physics canvas with bodies but no way to interact or no scoring/end state

guidelines.rule: Wire the engine in exactly this order and never skip a piece: (1) const engine = Engine.create(); (2) const render = Render.create({ element: document.body, engine, options: { width: innerWidth, height: innerHeight, pixelRatio: Math.min(devicePixelRatio||1, 2), wireframes: false, background: '#0b0e1a' } }); Render.run(render); (3) const runner = Runner.create(); Runner.run(runner, engine);. The three Run/Render/Runner calls together (Render.run draws, Runner.run steps physics) are what make bodies appear AND move. A common dead-canvas bug is calling Engine.create() and adding bodies but forgetting Render.run(render) or Runner.run(runner, engine), so nothing draws or nothing moves.

ALWAYS set wireframes:false in Render.create options. The matter.js default is wireframes:true, which renders only thin green outlines on a transparent black canvas and looks broken/unfinished. With wireframes:false the renderer uses each body's render.fillStyle/strokeStyle so you get solid colored shapes. Set a real background color too (e.g. background:'#0b0e1a'); leaving it default looks like a blank failed render.

Add bodies to the world with Composite.add(engine.world, [bodyA, bodyB, ...]) and remove with Composite.remove(engine.world, body). Do NOT use the deprecated World.add / World.addBody / World.remove APIs - they are removed/renamed in matter.js 0.19.x and either throw 'World.add is not a function' or silently no-op so nothing ever enters the world and the canvas stays empty. Read bodies back with Composite.allBodies(engine.world).

Size and keep the render canvas matched to the viewport on init AND on resize. On init set render.canvas.width = innerWidth; render.canvas.height = innerHeight; render.options.width = innerWidth; render.options.height = innerHeight;. In a window 'resize' listener, update all four of those plus Render.lookAt(render, { min:{x:0,y:0}, max:{x:innerWidth,y:innerHeight} }) (or reset render.bounds) and reposition the boundary walls. A fixed 800x600 canvas leaves dead margins and pushes the walls off-screen so bodies escape; CSS must include html,body{margin:0;overflow:hidden} canvas{display:block}.

Build every body with the Matter.Bodies factory helpers, never as hand-written plain objects. Use Bodies.rectangle(x, y, w, h, opts), Bodies.circle(x, y, r, opts), Bodies.polygon(x, y, sides, r, opts), Bodies.trapezoid(...). For stacks/walls/pyramids use Composites.stack(x, y, cols, rows, gapX, gapY, (px,py)=>Bodies.rectangle(px,py,40,40)). Hand-built objects like { position:{x,y}, vertices:[...] } lack proper inertia, bounds, and collision geometry, so the solver silently fails and bodies phase through each other or never collide.

Enclose the scene with four STATIC boundary walls so nothing falls forever, and make the floor thick to stop fast bodies tunneling. const w=innerWidth, h=innerHeight; const floor = Bodies.rectangle(w/2, h+40, w, 120, { isStatic:true }); const ceil = Bodies.rectangle(w/2, -40, w, 80, { isStatic:true }); const left = Bodies.rectangle(-40, h/2, 80, h*3, { isStatic:true }); const right = Bodies.rectangle(w+40, h/2, 80, h*3, { isStatic:true }); Composite.add(engine.world, [floor, ceil, left, right]);. Reposition all four on every resize. A floor only a few px thick lets a fast body skip through it in one step - use 80-120px or raise engine.positionIterations/velocityIterations.

Cap the live body count and cull bodies that leave the play area so the solver never grinds to a halt. const MAX_BODIES = (innerWidth < 700) ? 120 : 300;. Before spawning, check Composite.allBodies(engine.world).length < MAX_BODIES. On Events.on(engine, 'afterUpdate', ...) iterate Composite.allBodies and Composite.remove(engine.world, b) any non-static body whose position.y > innerHeight + 200 (fell out) or that is far off-screen. Spawning balls forever with no cap and no cull is the #1 cause of frame-rate collapse in plinko/bouncy-pit toys.

Run physics on the fixed-timestep Runner, not a raw rAF with variable delta. const runner = Runner.create(); Runner.run(runner, engine); advances the engine at a stable ~16.6ms step. Do NOT write function loop(){ Engine.update(engine, performance.now()-last); requestAnimationFrame(loop); } with a measured variable delta - that destabilizes the solver and makes bodies tunnel through walls and jitter. If you must call Engine.update manually, pass a FIXED delta of 1000/60 (about 16.667), never the real elapsed time. Stop with Runner.stop(runner) on teardown.

Wire drag-and-fling with Matter.Mouse + Matter.MouseConstraint for both mouse and touch. const mouse = Mouse.create(render.canvas); const mc = MouseConstraint.create(engine, { mouse, constraint: { stiffness: 0.2, render: { visible: false } } }); Composite.add(engine.world, mc); render.mouse = mouse;. Then set mouse.pixelRatio = render.options.pixelRatio (otherwise the grab point is offset from the click on Retina). Add CSS canvas{touch-action:none} so touch-drag grabs a body instead of scrolling/zooming the page; without it touch-drag either does nothing or pans the page and the ragdoll/catapult is unplayable on phones.

Pre-spawn an interactive scene on first load and drive scoring from collision events, not position polling. In setup spawn the initial bodies (pegs + a few pucks for plinko, a built tower for topple, dominoes for a cascade) and show a score starting at 0 plus a clear DROP/LAUNCH/GO control (also bind the space bar) and a RESET button that calls Composite.clear(engine.world, false) (keep statics) or rebuilds the world. Detect scoring with Events.on(engine, 'collisionStart', ev => ev.pairs.forEach(p => { /* match p.bodyA.label / p.bodyB.label */ }));, tagging bodies via the label option at creation (Bodies.circle(x,y,r,{ label:'puck' })). A loaded-but-inert canvas with no way to act reads as broken even though it rendered.

Give bodies real material values so the physics feels intentional, and set per-body fill colors via the render option. Pass restitution (bounciness), friction, and density: bouncy balls Bodies.circle(x,y,r,{ restitution:0.85, friction:0.02, render:{ fillStyle:'#ff3ea5' } }); dominoes/stacked blocks Bodies.rectangle(x,y,12,60,{ restitution:0.05, friction:0.6, render:{ fillStyle:'#ffd23f' } }). Without restitution everything lands dead with no bounce; without per-body render.fillStyle (and with wireframes:false) bodies render in a default flat color and the scene looks monochrome. For a floaty feel lower engine.gravity.y (e.g. 0.5).
